item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn’t it be Safe class? Given that, despite housing sentient creatures, its anomalous properties don’t manifest until someone enters it?

item.title: Image Credit
item.content:encoded: https://picryl.com/media/holister-mo-aug-15-2013-this-mobile-home-is-uninhabitable-after-a-flash-flood-fea336

item.title: Re: Image
item.content:encoded: Image has been updated. Thank you!

item.title: Image
item.content:encoded:
as a direct result of Scantron's "Lost and Foundation" podcast, I researched this image and discovered that it is a derivative of Katharina Fritsch's 1988 work "The Dinner Party".

It is, as surmised on the podcast, not remotely Creative Commons-compatible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1992 was found on 9/18/1995, when agents investigating reports of anomalous activity from ██████ ███████.

This seems strangely worded to me. Maybe instead of "when agents investigating" it should be "when agents were investigating?"

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

So, it was pointed out up thread that SCP-1813 is similar to SCP-1992 in that both provide ways of viewing numerous potential actions/outcomes via various copies of the subject. Its likely this similarity would be commented on in the documentation of at least one of those articles. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

Instances of SCP-1992-1 are simplified humanoids, superficially resembling the subject. Moving between rooms within SCP-1992 causes one instance of SCP-1992-1 to manifest at each available exit, which will then act out a situation that could have occurred if the subject had moved in that direction.

To this:

Instances of SCP-1992-1 are simplified humanoids, superficially resembling the subject. Moving between rooms within SCP-1992 causes one instance of SCP-1992-1 to manifest at each available exit, which will then act out a situation that could have occurred if the subject had moved in that direction, similar to the effects of long term exposure to SCP-1813.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: fyre18, slow down and actually fully read the articles you're commenting on. Your responses are short, spammy, and not helpful. Continuation will result in revocation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I concur to TL333s. The interview was good. Even though the SCPs responses are quite annoying. I did like the picture as well.

item.title: Great +1
item.content:encoded: This is great. I absolutely love the interview log and how the instance plays with grammar to really drive home the whole "Hive Mind" concept. I think the concept is really solid and one that so many have tried (and failed) in the past. This leaves just the perfect amount to the imagination and leave a lot of room for theories and speculation. Fantastic, gave me chills.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The main entrance to SCP-1992 has been fitted with standard security devices, with access authorized for appropriately ranked personnel.

I believe that's common sense part of standard containment procedures and should not be detailed in special procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the creepiness. But…I could lie…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But… What about 2 or more subjects entering SCP-1992? This has not even been suggested yet!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Impressive. Well done. +1 from me.

I should note that the "simplification as it gets bigger" makes this entire skip a metaphor for Floating Point decimal/rounding errors, which were responsible for Minecraft's infamous Far Lands. Which is kinda funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Despite painfully awkward wording, I really like this.

I expected a little more horrorscary grimdark, and I kinda appreciate the fact that you didn't take that approach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heck yeah quantum suicide! I like it. The interview log definitely did it for me. opens the possibility for something MUCH more sinister. upvote.

item.title: Re: Isn't this an issue?
item.content:encoded: You can have my +1 now

item.title: Re: Isn't this an issue?
item.content:encoded:
It says it right…

Oh, dicks, I left out the bit that actually stated that, that explains the confusion.

Made a small edit, makes that more explicit now.

item.title: Re: Isn't this an issue?
item.content:encoded: Where does it say this? I keep missing that detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is strangely 184-ish. I like 184…
*compute*

item.title: Re: Isn't this an issue?
item.content:encoded: They disappear when the subject leaves, and it starts over when someone else goes in.

item.title: Isn't this an issue?
item.content:encoded:
If SCP-1992-1 self-replicates, wouldn't the insides of SCP-1992 be unimaginably huge and overpopulated by now? You imply that the instances of SCP-1992-1 only exist temporarily, but it isn't explicitly stated, so I'm picturing a keter-cake scenario in there.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think SCP-1813 pulled this off better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know that visual illusion that happens when you stand between two parallel mirror? That has always freaked me out. This SCP is that a magnitude greater. +1 for making a cool effect creepier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"We could answer the man. We could remain silent. We could ask the man the same. We could lie."

+1. At first I wasn't even going to vote but damn that is clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Confusing and convoluted as all hell, but I was able to follow it and I think the interview was a great touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this creepy. Maybe because of this. I think there's actually no corelation between the song and my feeling of creepyness while reading this.

Well, I could lie…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"We could lie."

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is a bit clearer now, but just boring. It would take a substantial log with much more interesting occurrences/conversations to bring the "all possibilities" trope to life— why make that your premise if you aren't going to really do anything with it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I suppose it's at least clear now. Still not a huge fan, but it's enough to cancel my downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did some editing to the description, hopefully things are a bit clearer now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahyup

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is… a quantum mobile home? You see all possibilities of your actions play out at once?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nicholas Cage will summarize.
Overall, I think that this is a strong idea but there isn't really much to it besides the interview, which is very good. It just doesn't seem to have much substance. I don't know what you would add, but there should be something more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… moving out in a parallel action in which an alternate decision had been made.

Whahh-?

I had to make myself read this four times to figure out what the SCP was/does. This reads like a very convoluted oral explanation of an SCP, and there was nothing in the log or the (somewhat unrelated-feeling) image to redeem the poor presentation. I don't even feel like I can adequately criticize the concept, because I'm still not 100% sure what it is or implies or could become.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The description of what this thing actually does is incredibly unclear, so much so that I still have no idea what the point of the thing is. The interview doesn't clear anything either. If this can be fixed to be clearer I might reconsider, but for now it's a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted for the reasons I gave Roget in chat. The core concept doesn't interest me and the interview log felt a little cheesy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If the subject crosses between the different rooms within SCP-1992, causing an instance of SCP-1992-1 to be generated from all other exits from the room, moving out in a parallel action in which an alternate decision had been made.

Come again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a collaboration between Roget and I, many thanks to him for his help.

Hope you all like it.
